perturbation.original_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information and it will not without the ex press prior consent of the Donor either: 1.1 communicate or otherwise make available the Information to any third party, other than: a) an employee of the Recipient who requires the Information in connection with his employment and then only if the employee is bound by conditions of secrecy no less strict that those set out in this Agreement which conditions Recipient hereby agrees to enforce at the request of the Donor ; and/or b) the UK Intellectual Property Office, the European Patent Office, the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the World Intellectual Property Organisation, and any other national or international intellectual property office worldwide , but only with respect to the Applications in accordance with the Purpose ; and/or c) any person in any jur isdiction worldwide who has been authorised by the Donor or Recipient to act on behalf of, or otherwise assist, the Donor with respect to one or more of the Applications , and any duly authorised legal representative of the Donor in any jurisdiction worldwi de; and/or 1.2 use the Information for any investigation, research, development or manufacture, other than so far as any such activity is essential for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information. 1.1 communicate or otherwise make available the Information to any third party; and/or 1.2 use the Information for any investigation, research, development or manufacture.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clauses regarding exceptions to confidentiality (1.1 a, b, c and 1.2 condition) creates an in-text contradiction. The agreement now broadly states the Recipient will preserve confidentiality, but eliminating the specific exceptions makes it unclear to whom the Recipient *can* disclose the information, or under which conditions the Recipient *can* make available the Information. Other sections reference the 'Purpose' which involves third parties, but the removal of these clauses introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information. 1.1 communicate or otherwise make available the Information to any third party; and/or 1.2 use the Information for any investigation, research, development or manufacture.
perturbation.justification: The original text outlines specific exceptions to the confidentiality of information, allowing disclosure to certain parties under defined conditions (employees, intellectual property offices, authorized individuals). The changed text removes these exceptions, broadly prohibiting the recipient from communicating or using the information, which contradicts other sections of the document that assume the recipient has some level of freedom to disclose information for the 'Purpose' of filing patent applications. This creates a direct conflict as the Recipient is obligated to work with 3rd parties to pursue 'the Applications' while the changed text restricts them from communicating or making available the Information to any third party or using it for any investigation, research, development or manufacture, for any reason, including activities that would be essential for the Purpose.

perturbation.original_text: 3 The above obligations shall also apply to any sample or article incorporating or derived from the Information and whether or not provided by the Donor (‘Samples’). 4 The Recipient shall, at any time and if so requested by the Donor, return to the Donor (or if the Donor so requests, destroy or erase) all Samples and any documents provided by the Donor recording the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3 The above obligations shall also apply to any sample or article incorporating or derived from the Information and whether or not provided by the Donor (‘Samples’). 4 The Recipient may, at any time, return to the Donor (or if the Donor so requests, destroy or erase) all Samples and any documents provided by the Donor recording the Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' in section 4 weakens the obligation to return or destroy samples upon request. Section 3 states that 'above obligations' apply to Samples, implying a mandatory return/destruction policy as initially stated. This change creates an in-text contradiction because it suggests that returning or destroying 'samples' and 'any documents' is now optional, contradicting the intent to preserve confidentiality when the Donor requires it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3 The above obligations shall also apply to any sample or article incorporating or derived from the Information and whether or not provided by the Donor (‘Samples’).
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Recipient 'shall' return or destroy Samples, making it mandatory. The changed text modifies this to 'may', making the return or destruction optional. The contradicted text explicitly states 'The above obligations shall also apply to any sample', referring back to the initially mandatory return/destroy policy. The change introduces a direct contradiction by making the action optional when the contradicted text implies it is obligatory, creating uncertainty about the recipient's responsibilities regarding the Samples.

perturbation.original_text: 2 The above obligations shall not apply or shall cease to apply to such of the Information as the Recipient can show to the reasonable satisfaction of the Donor: 2.1 has become public knowledge other than through the fault of the Recipient; or 2.2 was already known to the Recipient prior to disclosure by the Donor; or 2.3 has been received from a third party who did not acquire it in confidence from the Donor or from someone owing a duty of confidence to the Donor.
perturbation.changed_text: 2 The above obligations shall not apply to such of the Information as the Recipient claims: 2.1 has become public knowledge; or 2.2 was already known to the Recipient prior to disclosure by the Donor; or 2.3 has been received from a third party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause '...to the reasonable satisfaction of the Donor' and '...other than through the fault of the Recipient' and '...who did not acquire it in confidence from the Donor or from someone owing a duty of confidence to the Donor' creates an in-text contradiction. The original text requires the Recipient to *prove* certain circumstances to be released from obligations. The edited text allows the Recipient's mere *claim* of such circumstances to suffice, even if the 'public knowledge' was through their fault or the information has been aquired in confidence, which directly contradicts the intent to protect the Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the Recipient to demonstrate 'to the reasonable satisfaction of the Donor' that the Information falls under exceptions (public knowledge, prior knowledge, third-party source). The original text also omits clauses that safeguard the information aquired in confidence. This contradicts the contradicted text's explicit undertaking by the Recipient to 'respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information', as the Recipient now only needs to 'claim' such circumstances, even if the information was released through the Recipient's fault. The change creates a clear contradiction, satisfying the condition for an Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: info@albright -ip-co.uk www.albright -ip.co.uk Tel: +44 (0) 1242 691801 Fax: +44 (0) 1242 692351 Non–Disclosure Agreement Date: this 1st April 2019 Parties: (1) ‘The Donor’: (2) ‘The Recipient’: Albright IP Limited , of County House, Bayshill Road, Cheltenham, GL50 3BA Operative Provisions:
perturbation.changed_text: Non–Disclosure Agreement Date: this 1st April 2019 Parties: (1) ‘The Donor’: (2) ‘The Recipient’: Operative Provisions:
perturbation.explanation: By removing the Donor's identifying information (Albright IP Limited, address, contact details), while still referring to 'The Donor' throughout the agreement, creates an in-text contradiction. It becomes unclear who exactly the Donor is, hindering the enforceability of the agreement. The agreement states rights and obligations of 'The Donor', but without clear identification, these are ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the identifying information of 'The Donor' (Albright IP Limited, address, contact details). The contradicted text refers to the 'Donor' disclosing information. Without the donor being clearly defined, the disclosure obligation becomes unclear and creates a direct conflict, as it is now uncertain who is disclosing the information.

perturbation.original_text: 5 The term of this agreement is to be five years from the first disclosure of the Information by the Donor to the Recipient , or until the first public disclosure of the Information, or until the Recipient is authorised to disclose the Information by the Donor to a third party (such as a Patent Office), whichever is shorter.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 The term of this agreement is indefinite.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the term to 'indefinite' directly contradicts the previous implicit assertion that the term would be no longer than five years or until first public disclosure. This creates uncertainty as the obligations are now open ended, even though the purpose of the agreement (patent application) has a limited, defined life-cycle.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined the term of the agreement as five years or until an earlier event. The changed text makes the term 'indefinite'. The contradicted text specifies that the information is disclosed for the purpose of patent applications. Patent applications have a limited lifecycle, creating an inconsistency with an indefinite term. By changing the agreement to indefinite, the recipient's obligations to confidentiality are now open-ended, even though the purpose of the agreement is of a finite duration, triggering a contradiction. This is further supported by the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 6 The agreement shall be governed by the courts of England and Wales, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales . SIGNED by the Donor: ........................................................................................................... ........................................................................................................... SIGNED for and on behalf of the Rec ipient: ........................................................................................................... Adrian Niall Hocking Patents Director | Chartered British Patent Attorney | European Patent Attorney
perturbation.changed_text: SIGNED by the Rec ipient: ........................................................................................................... Adrian Niall Hocking Patents Director | Chartered British Patent Attorney | European Patent Attorney
perturbation.explanation: Removing the choice of law and forum selection clause ('The agreement shall be governed by the courts of England and Wales, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales') creates an in-text contradiction, since now it is not clear which juridiction can be used to solve possible future disagreements or legal issues.
perturbation.contradicted_text: SIGNED by the Rec ipient: ........................................................................................................... Adrian Niall Hocking Patents Director | Chartered British Patent Attorney | European Patent Attorney
perturbation.justification: The original text specified the governing law and jurisdiction as the courts of England and Wales. The changed text omits this clause, creating an in-text contradiction. The contradiction arises because without a governing law clause, it is unclear which jurisdiction's laws apply to the agreement, potentially conflicting with any implied assumptions or expectations regarding jurisdiction within the contract. Since the contradicted text is the same as the changed text location, it showcases that there is no current selection of law selected in that area, hence a contradiction. This meets the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction', because essential language was removed, creating a contradiction.
